parts.homeLangDetails: From geven (to give) + -er (agent suffix). A 'work-giver' is the one who gives you work — your employer. The same root appears in vergeven (to forgive) and cadeau geven (to give a gift).

words.exampleSentence: Mijn werkgever biedt goede arbeidsvoorwaarden aan.
words.exampleTranslation: My employer offers good working conditions.

parts.homeLangDetails: A room or chamber. Cognate with English 'chamber' and German Kammer. In Dutch compounds it typically means a specific room, as in woonkamer (living room) or badkamer (bathroom).

words.exampleSentence: De slaapkamer heeft een groot raam.
words.exampleTranslation: The bedroom has a large window.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Greek biblion (book). The same root appears in 'bibliography' and 'Bible.'
From Greek theke (a place for storing things). You also see this in 'discotheek' (discotheque — a place for records) and 'apotheek' (pharmacy — originally a storehouse).

words.exampleSentence: Ik haal elke week nieuwe boeken op uit de bibliotheek.
words.exampleTranslation: I pick up new books from the library every week.

parts.homeLangDetails: The Dutch negating prefix, equivalent to English un-, in-, or im-. It flips the meaning of whatever follows: mogelijk (possible) becomes onmogelijk (impossible), eerlijk (fair) becomes oneerlijk (unfair).
From the verb mogen (to be allowed to, to be able to). This root carries the sense of possibility or permission — the same idea as in 'may' or 'might' in English.
A suffix that turns nouns or roots into adjectives (and often adverbs too). Similar to English -like or -ly: vriendelijk (friendly), gevaarlijk (dangerous), natuurlijk (naturally).

words.exampleSentence: Het is onmogelijk om dit in één dag te doen.
words.exampleTranslation: It is impossible to do this in one day.

parts.homeLangDetails: A suffix forming nouns that express a state, condition, or relationship — directly equivalent to English -ship. Vriendschap = friendship, broederschap = brotherhood, maatschappij (society) shares the same origin.

words.exampleSentence: Hun vriendschap begon al op de basisschool.
words.exampleTranslation: Their friendship began back in primary school.

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix that often makes a verb transitive or adds a sense of 'thoroughly doing' something. Here it turns grijpen (to grasp, to seize) into begrijpen (to understand) — mentally grasping something completely.

From grijpen (to grab, to seize). The same root appears in aangrijpen (to affect deeply) and grijpbaar (tangible). 'To understand' is literally 'to grasp' — a metaphor shared with English comprehend (Latin com + prehendere = seize together).

words.translationLong: to understand, to comprehend, to grasp
words.exampleSentence: Ik begrijp niet wat je bedoelt.
words.exampleTranslation: I don't understand what you mean.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here ver- signals a change into a new condition — making something better. It works similarly in vergroten (to enlarge), versnellen (to speed up), and verlengen (to extend). Think of it as a causative 'make it more so.'

words.translationLong: to improve, to get better, to correct
words.exampleSentence: Ze probeert elke dag haar Nederlands te verbeteren.
words.exampleTranslation: She tries to improve her Dutch every day.

parts.homeLangDetails: Ont- is a tricky prefix. Its core sense is separation or the start of something, but in ontvangen it expresses reception — taking something that comes toward you. Think of it as 'catching what comes off' someone else. The same root gives us 'receive' via Latin recipere.
From vangen (to catch, to capture). Appears in opvangen (to catch/absorb), vangnet (safety net), and gevangen (captured, imprisoned).

words.translationLong: to receive, to accept, to welcome
words.exampleSentence: Hij ontving gisteren een belangrijk pakket.
words.exampleTranslation: He received an important package yesterday.

parts.homeLangDetails: Her- is the Dutch 'do it again' prefix, directly equivalent to English re-. Herschrijven = rewrite, herstellen = restore, herzien = revise. It always implies returning to or repeating an action.
From schrijven (to write). The root shifts to schrijf- in compounds. Cognate with English 'scribe' via Latin scribere. Also in beschrijven (to describe) and voorschrijven (to prescribe).

words.translationLong: to rewrite, to rewrite from scratch
words.exampleSentence: De auteur moest het hele hoofdstuk herschrijven.
words.exampleTranslation: The author had to rewrite the entire chapter.

parts.homeLangDetails: An adjective meaning clear, evident, or obvious. It is itself built from duid- (to indicate, to point out, related to duiden) + -elijk (-ly/-like). So at its core: 'in a way that points things out clearly.'
A suffix that turns adjectives into abstract nouns, equivalent to English -ness or -hood. Duidelijkheid = clarity, vriendelijkheid = friendliness, eerlijkheid = honesty.

words.exampleSentence: Ze sprak met grote duidelijkheid over het probleem.
words.exampleTranslation: She spoke about the problem with great clarity.

parts.homeLangDetails: Boodschap means both 'message' and 'errand.' In the plural, boodschappen specifically means 'groceries' or 'shopping' — the errands you run to get supplies. Cognate with English 'bishop' via Old English bodscipe (message-bearer)... but that connection is very distant!
The Dutch plural suffix -en. Here boodschap becomes boodschappen (errands/groceries) before compounding with lijst.

words.exampleSentence: Vergeet niet de boodschappenlijst mee te nemen.
words.exampleTranslation: Don't forget to bring the shopping list.

parts.homeLangDetails: From staan (to stand). This root captures the idea of a position or stance — something that stands on its own. The same root is in toestand (condition, literally 'approach-stand'), bestand (file/archive), and omstandigheden (circumstances).
A common Dutch adjectival suffix equivalent to English -y, -ful, or -ous. It turns nouns or roots into adjectives: zelfstandig (independent), krachtig (powerful), moedig (brave/courageous).

words.exampleSentence: Ze is heel zelfstandig en regelt alles zelf.
words.exampleTranslation: She is very independent and arranges everything herself.

words.translationLong: to cooperate, to collaborate, to work together
words.exampleSentence: De twee bedrijven besloten nauw samen te werken.
words.exampleTranslation: The two companies decided to work closely together.

parts.homeLangDetails: A separable prefix meaning 'out' or 'outward.' In uitleggen, it gives the sense of 'laying something out' — spreading it open so it can be seen and understood. Compare uitspreiden (to spread out) and uitpakken (to unpack).

From leggen (to lay, to place). The idea of 'laying out' an explanation is shared with English: we also 'lay out' an argument or 'explain' (from Latin explanare = flatten out). See also neerleggen (to put down) and vastleggen (to record/fix).

The infinitive ending -en, with the preceding g belonging to the root leggen. Leggen doubles the g before the ending, so the split is leg + gen rather than legg + en — but functionally this is just the infinitive marker.

words.translationLong: to explain, to lay out, to clarify
words.exampleSentence: Kun jij me uitleggen hoe dit werkt?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you explain to me how this works?

parts.homeLangDetails: A separable prefix with several senses. In aantrekken it can mean 'to put on' (clothes) or 'to attract' — both involve bringing something toward or onto yourself. The 'on' sense appears in aandoen (to turn on), the 'attract' sense in aantrekkelijk (attractive).

From trekken (to pull, to draw, to move). A versatile root: optrekken (to move along with), uittrekken (to pull off), wegtrekken (to move away). The same root gives Dutch trek (the urge or pull toward something, as in 'I have a trek for adventure').
The infinitive ending -en, with the k belonging to the root trekken. Just as with uitleggen, the final consonant of the root doubles, so the visible split is trek + ken rather than trekk + en.

words.translationLong: to attract, to draw in; to put on (clothing)
words.exampleSentence: De stad trekt elk jaar veel toeristen aan.
words.exampleTranslation: The city attracts many tourists every year.

parts.homeLangDetails: A separable prefix that in this context gives the sense of 'reaching out' or 'calling up' — similar to how English uses 'up' in 'ring up' or 'call up' someone.

words.translationLong: to call, to ring up, to phone
words.exampleSentence: Ik zal je morgen opbellen.
words.exampleTranslation: I will call you tomorrow.

parts.homeLangDetails: A separable prefix meaning 'off' or 'away'. Here it conveys the sense of washing something off — removing dirt or grease from dishes.

words.translationLong: to wash up, to do the dishes, to wash off
words.exampleSentence: Na het eten ga ik afwassen.
words.exampleTranslation: After dinner I will do the dishes.

parts.homeLangDetails: A separable prefix meaning 'along with' or 'together'. It implies bringing or taking someone or something with you — like 'take along' in English.

words.translationLong: to take with, to bring along, to take away
words.exampleSentence: Vergeet je paraplu niet mee te nemen.
words.exampleTranslation: Don't forget to take your umbrella along.

parts.homeLangDetails: A separable prefix meaning 'through' or 'on'. Here it conveys continuing or going on — carrying through with something, similar to 'carry on' or 'go through with it' in English.

words.translationLong: to continue, to go on, to proceed, to take place
words.exampleSentence: Het concert gaat door, ondanks de regen.
words.exampleTranslation: The concert is going ahead, despite the rain.

parts.homeLangDetails: A separable prefix meaning 'in' or 'into'. Here it gives the sense of filling something in — like writing into a form or blank space.

words.translationLong: to fill in, to fill out, to complete (a form)
words.exampleSentence: Kun je dit formulier invullen?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you fill in this form?

parts.homeLangDetails: An adjectival suffix similar to English '-y' or '-ful', turning a noun into an adjective. So geluk (luck/happiness) + -ig = lucky, happy.

words.exampleSentence: Ze was erg gelukkig met haar nieuwe baan.
words.exampleTranslation: She was very happy with her new job.

parts.homeLangDetails: An adjectival suffix similar to English '-y' or '-ful'. Combined with on- (un-) and geluk (happiness), it gives 'unhappy' or 'unfortunate'.

words.exampleSentence: Hij voelde zich erg ongelukkig na het nieuws.
words.exampleTranslation: He felt very unhappy after the news.

parts.homeLangDetails: An adjectival suffix meaning '-like' or '-ly', similar to German -lich. Eerlijk literally means 'honor-like', i.e., honorable — and by extension, honest.
A nominalizing suffix equivalent to English '-ness' or '-hood', turning adjectives into abstract nouns. So eerlijk (honest) + -heid = honesty.

words.exampleSentence: Eerlijkheid duurt het langst.
words.exampleTranslation: Honesty is the best policy.

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix that often makes a verb transitive or gives it a sense of applying to an object. Here it transforms 'trouwen' (to trust) into 'vertrouwen/betrouwen' — to place trust in something.
A suffix equivalent to English '-able', meaning 'capable of being' or 'worthy of'. So betrouwbaar = trust-able, i.e., reliable.

words.exampleSentence: Hij is een betrouwbare vriend.
words.exampleTranslation: He is a reliable friend.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root duur can mean both 'expensive/dear' and 'lasting/enduring'. In duurzaamheid the sense is 'lasting' or 'enduring' — something that persists over time.
A suffix similar to English '-some' or '-ful', giving the adjective a sense of 'having the quality of'. Duurzaam = enduring, sustainable.
A nominalizing suffix equivalent to English '-ness', turning adjectives into abstract nouns. Duurzaam (sustainable) + -heid = sustainability.

words.exampleSentence: Duurzaamheid is belangrijk voor de toekomst van onze planeet.
words.exampleTranslation: Sustainability is important for the future of our planet.

parts.homeLangDetails: A separable prefix meaning 'out'. In uitvinden, it gives the sense of 'finding out' or 'devising' something — bringing something new out into the world.
A nominalizing suffix that turns a verb into a noun, similar to English '-ing' or '-tion'. Uitvinden (to invent) → uitvinding (invention).

words.exampleSentence: De uitvinding van het wiel veranderde de wereld.
words.exampleTranslation: The invention of the wheel changed the world.

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix that makes the verb transitive — you 'write on' or 'write about' something. Beschrijven means to describe, literally to write upon/about something.
A nominalizing suffix turning a verb into a noun. Beschrijven (to describe) → beschrijving (description).

words.exampleSentence: De beschrijving van het huis klonk erg aantrekkelijk.
words.exampleTranslation: The description of the house sounded very attractive.

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix that here signals a change of state or transformation — turning something into something else. Veranderen means to change or alter.
A nominalizing suffix turning a verb into a noun. Veranderen (to change) → verandering (change, alteration).

words.exampleSentence: Er is een grote verandering in zijn gedrag.
words.exampleTranslation: There is a big change in his behavior.

parts.homeLangDetails: A separable prefix meaning 'up' or 'open'. In oplossen, it gives the sense of dissolving or loosening up a problem — resolving it.
A nominalizing suffix turning a verb into a noun. Oplossen (to solve/dissolve) → oplossing (solution).

words.exampleSentence: We moeten een oplossing vinden voor dit probleem.
words.exampleTranslation: We need to find a solution to this problem.

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix meaning 'again' or 're-', equivalent to English 're-'. Herhalen = to repeat, literally to fetch/pull again.
A nominalizing suffix turning a verb into a noun. Herhalen (to repeat) → herhaling (repetition, repeat).

words.exampleSentence: Herhaling is de moeder van alle leren.
words.exampleTranslation: Repetition is the mother of all learning.

words.exampleSentence: Hij speelt elke week voetbal met zijn vrienden.
words.exampleTranslation: He plays football with his friends every week.

parts.homeLangDetails: From vliegen (to fly). The same root appears in vliegtuig (airplane) and vliegeren (to fly a kite).

words.exampleSentence: We moeten morgen vroeg naar het vliegveld.
words.exampleTranslation: We have to go to the airport early tomorrow.

parts.homeLangDetails: A linking element between the two parts of this compound, derived from the plural form of krant (kranten). It carries no independent meaning here — it just connects the two halves smoothly.
From lezen (to read). The root lez- appears here before the agent suffix -er.

words.exampleSentence: Hij is een trouwe krantenlezer die elke ochtend de krant leest.
words.exampleTranslation: He is a loyal newspaper reader who reads the paper every morning.

words.exampleSentence: Ze kocht een roman in de boekwinkel op de hoek.
words.exampleTranslation: She bought a novel at the bookshop on the corner.

parts.homeLangDetails: From weren (to ward off, to defend against). This is not the common word weer meaning 'weather' or 'again' — it's an older word meaning resistance or defense. So brandweer literally means 'fire defense.'

words.exampleSentence: De brandweer was snel ter plaatse bij de brand.
words.exampleTranslation: The fire brigade arrived quickly at the scene of the fire.

parts.homeLangDetails: From leiden (to lead, to guide) + the nominalizing suffix -ing. Leiding on its own means 'leadership' or 'guidance,' but in compounds like this one it takes on the sense of a conduit — something that guides a substance from one place to another. Think of it as a 'guiding channel.'

words.translationLong: water pipe, water main, water supply system
words.exampleSentence: Er is een lek in de waterleiding onder de straat.
words.exampleTranslation: There is a leak in the water pipe under the street.

words.exampleSentence: Het hoofdkantoor van het bedrijf is gevestigd in Amsterdam.
words.exampleTranslation: The company's head office is located in Amsterdam.

parts.homeLangDetails: From rijden (to ride, to drive). In this context it specifically refers to driving a vehicle. The same root appears in rijden, rijstrook (driving lane), and rijschool (driving school).
From bewijzen (to prove). A bewijs is a piece of evidence or a document that proves something. So rijbewijs is literally a 'proof of driving' — a certificate showing you're licensed to drive.

words.exampleSentence: Mag ik uw rijbewijs zien, meneer?
words.exampleTranslation: May I see your driver's license, sir?

words.exampleSentence: Alle reservesleutels hangen in de sleutelkast bij de ingang.
words.exampleTranslation: All spare keys hang in the key cabinet near the entrance.

parts.homeLangDetails: From zetten (to set, to place, to make). Koffiezetten is a common Dutch phrase meaning 'to make coffee' — you 'set' the coffee rather than 'brew' it. The verb zetten here carries the specific sense of preparing a hot drink.

The agent suffix -er attached to the verb stem zet-, but spelled -ter after the t in zet to avoid doubling. This suffix forms nouns meaning 'one who does' or 'device that does' — here, a machine that makes coffee.

words.exampleSentence: De koffiezetter staat elke ochtend al klaar als ik wakker word.
words.exampleTranslation: The coffee maker is already ready every morning when I wake up.

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix that makes the verb transitive or gives it a sense of encompassing. Here it helps form begrijpen (to understand, literally to 'grasp around').

From grijpen (to grab, to grasp). Understanding is conceptualized as mentally grasping something — the same root appears in English 'grip' and 'gripe.' Compare German begreifen (to comprehend), which works the same way.

words.exampleSentence: De uitleg van de leraar was heel begrijpelijk.
words.exampleTranslation: The teacher's explanation was very understandable.

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix that makes the verb transitive or gives it a sense of encompassing. Here it helps form begrijpen (to understand, literally to 'grasp around').

From grijpen (to grab, to grasp). Understanding is conceptualized as mentally grasping something — the same root appears in English 'grip' and 'gripe.' Compare German begreifen (to comprehend), which works the same way.

words.exampleSentence: Zijn gedrag was volkomen onbegrijpelijk voor iedereen.
words.exampleTranslation: His behavior was completely incomprehensible to everyone.

words.exampleSentence: Het virus is onzichtbaar voor het blote oog.
words.exampleTranslation: The virus is invisible to the naked eye.

parts.homeLangDetails: From beheersen (to control, to master, to manage). Beheer on its own means 'management' or 'administration.' The root is related to heer (lord, master) — to beheer something is to have mastery over it.
A linking element between beheer and -baar, added for ease of pronunciation. It carries no independent meaning.

words.exampleSentence: De situatie was moeilijk maar nog net beheersbaar.
words.exampleTranslation: The situation was difficult but still just manageable.

parts.homeLangDetails: From passen (to fit, to suit) and aanpassen (to adapt, to adjust). The core idea is fitting something to something else — like tailoring clothes to fit a person. Aanpassen means to make something fit its new context.

words.exampleSentence: Het systeem is aanpasbaar aan de wensen van de gebruiker.
words.exampleTranslation: The system is adaptable to the wishes of the user.

words.exampleSentence: Het is gevaarlijk om zonder helm te fietsen.
words.exampleTranslation: It is dangerous to cycle without a helmet.

words.exampleSentence: De onveiligheid in de wijk baart veel bewoners zorgen.
words.exampleTranslation: The insecurity in the neighborhood worries many residents.

parts.homeLangDetails: A Dutch suffix that forms adjectives meaning 'characterized by' or 'inclined toward' something. Like English -some or -ful, it turns a noun into a quality: zorg (care) → zorgzaam (caring/careful).

words.exampleSentence: De zorgzaamheid van de verpleegster was indrukwekkend.
words.exampleTranslation: The nurse's caring nature was impressive.

parts.homeLangDetails: A very common Dutch (and German) prefix that often turns a noun or adjective into a fuller, more active quality. Here it reinforces 'hulp' — being actively disposed toward helping, not just capable of it.
A Dutch suffix meaning 'characterized by' or 'inclined toward' — like English -some or -ful. Combined with hulp (help), it gives 'inclined to help.'

words.exampleSentence: Ze is altijd behulpzaam als iemand een vraag heeft.
words.exampleTranslation: She is always helpful when someone has a question.

words.exampleSentence: Zijn vriendelijkheid maakt hem geliefd bij iedereen.
words.exampleTranslation: His friendliness makes him popular with everyone.

parts.homeLangDetails: A linking element between compound parts, common in Dutch and German. It has no independent meaning — it's just a connective sound that makes the compound easier to say.

words.translationLong: lifelong, for the duration of one's life
words.exampleSentence: Hij werd veroordeeld tot levenslang.
words.exampleTranslation: He was sentenced to life imprisonment.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here -elijk functions as an adjectival suffix meaning 'of the nature of' or 'relating to.' So tijdelijk means something like 'of a time-bound nature' — temporary, not permanent.

words.exampleSentence: Dit is slechts een tijdelijke oplossing.
words.exampleTranslation: This is only a temporary solution.

words.exampleSentence: Het zal waarschijnlijk morgen regenen.
words.exampleTranslation: It will probably rain tomorrow.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here -elijk carries the sense of 'worthy of' or 'deserving to be' — so opmerkelijk means something worthy of being noticed or remarked upon.

words.exampleSentence: Zijn herstel was opmerkelijk snel.
words.exampleTranslation: His recovery was remarkably fast.

parts.homeLangDetails: A common Dutch adjectival suffix, similar to English -y or -ous. It turns a noun phrase into an adjective: 'same time' → 'at the same time / simultaneous.'

words.exampleSentence: De twee evenementen vonden gelijktijdig plaats.
words.exampleTranslation: The two events took place simultaneously.

words.translationLong: meanwhile, in the meantime, in the interim
words.exampleSentence: Ze werkte, en ondertussen kookte hij het eten.
words.exampleTranslation: She worked, and meanwhile he cooked the food.

parts.homeLangDetails: A preposition-like particle meaning 'at' or 'in' that anchors this compound in time. Think of it like 'at the same time' — te sets up the temporal frame.
A linking element between gelijk and tijd in this compound. It has no meaning of its own — it's purely a connective that makes the word flow more naturally.

words.exampleSentence: Hij probeert nooit twee dingen tegelijkertijd te doen.
words.exampleTranslation: He never tries to do two things at the same time.

words.translationLong: in hindsight, afterwards, after the fact
words.exampleSentence: Achteraf gezien hadden we een andere keuze moeten maken.
words.exampleTranslation: In hindsight, we should have made a different choice.

words.translationLong: at the top, at the head of
words.exampleSentence: Zijn naam staat bovenaan de lijst.
words.exampleTranslation: His name is at the top of the list.

words.translationLong: at the bottom, at the foot of
words.exampleSentence: Zijn naam staat onderaan de lijst.
words.exampleTranslation: His name is at the bottom of the list.

parts.homeLangDetails: From gaan (to go). Gang shows up in many Dutch compounds to mean a movement, passage, or course of events — like hallway (literally a 'going-place') or the course of something happening.

words.exampleSentence: We zien veel vooruitgang in de wetenschap.
words.exampleTranslation: We see a lot of progress in science.

parts.homeLangDetails: From gaan (to go). The same root as in vooruitgang — gang captures the idea of movement or the course of something. Here, paired with achter-uit (backward-out), it becomes a 'going backward.'

words.exampleSentence: Er is een duidelijke achteruitgang in zijn gezondheid.
words.exampleTranslation: There is a clear decline in his health.

parts.homeLangDetails: From omgeven (to surround, literally 'to give around'). The root is geven (to give), which in this context carries the sense of enclosing or encompassing something.

words.exampleSentence: De omgeving van de stad is erg groen.
words.exampleTranslation: The surroundings of the city are very green.

parts.homeLangDetails: From leven (to live). The core root stripped of its infinitive ending, used here to form a noun via the -ing suffix.

words.exampleSentence: In een gezonde samenleving wordt iedereen gerespecteerd.
words.exampleTranslation: In a healthy society, everyone is respected.

parts.homeLangDetails: Related to 'common' or 'shared' — think of German gemein or English 'mean' in its older sense of 'held in common.' In modern Dutch gemeen can also mean 'mean/nasty,' but in compounds like this one it retains its older sense of shared or communal.

The Dutch equivalent of the English suffix -ship (as in friendship, fellowship). It turns an adjective or noun into an abstract concept denoting a state, condition, or collective. Compare: vriendschap (friendship), broederschap (brotherhood).

words.exampleSentence: De lokale gemeenschap organiseert elk jaar een festival.
words.exampleTranslation: The local community organizes a festival every year.

parts.homeLangDetails: The standard Dutch abstract noun suffix, equivalent to English -ness or -hood. Here it turns over (above, authority) into an abstract entity — 'the state of being above/in authority,' i.e., the governing body.

words.exampleSentence: De overheid heeft nieuwe maatregelen aangekondigd.
words.exampleTranslation: The government has announced new measures.

words.exampleSentence: De werkloosheid is dit jaar gedaald.
words.exampleTranslation: Unemployment has decreased this year.

parts.homeLangDetails: In vertrouwen, the ver- prefix intensifies or gives direction to the root trouwen (to trust, to marry). Here it conveys a settled, directed sense of trust — not just trust as a feeling, but trust placed in something.
Related to trouw (faithful, loyal) and the verb trouwen (to trust, to marry). The core idea is faithfulness and reliance — trusting something to be true or steady.

words.exampleSentence: Ze heeft veel zelfvertrouwen tijdens haar presentaties.
words.exampleTranslation: She has a lot of self-confidence during her presentations.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here ver- lends a sense of formal accountability — turning antwoorden (to answer) into the idea of being answerable, of having to account for something.
The same word as antwoord (answer). Responsibility in Dutch is literally built on the idea of 'having to answer for' something — the same logic as English 'answerable.'

words.exampleSentence: Hij neemt zijn verantwoordelijkheid serieus.
words.exampleTranslation: He takes his responsibility seriously.

parts.homeLangDetails: An adjective meaning conscious or aware. It comes from the prefix be- (making something present to oneself) + wust, related to weten (to know). So bewust literally suggests 'having made something known to oneself.'

The infinitive/noun form of zijn (to be). In Dutch compound nouns, zijn often functions like the English '-ness' or '-ness of being' — turning an adjective into the state of being that thing. Compare: welzijn (well-being), vrijheid/vrijzijn.

words.exampleSentence: Bewustzijn van je eigen gevoelens is heel belangrijk.
words.exampleTranslation: Awareness of your own feelings is very important.

parts.homeLangDetails: The Dutch prefix her- is a close cousin of English 're-' — it signals doing something again or returning to a previous state. In herinneren it means recalling something back to mind.

words.exampleSentence: Ik heb mooie herinneringen aan mijn jeugd.
words.exampleTranslation: I have fond memories of my youth.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix be- here is causative — it turns known (known/familiar) into the act of making something known. It transforms an adjective into an action directed outward.
From kennen (to know someone/something) — the same root as bekend (known, famous). This root is about familiarity and recognition, as distinct from weten (to know facts).

words.exampleSentence: De bekendmaking van de winnaar vond gisteren plaats.
words.exampleTranslation: The announcement of the winner took place yesterday.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix ont- often signals uncovering, removal, or the beginning of a process. Here it gives the sense of 'uncovering' — removing what was hidden to reveal something new.
From dekken (to cover). The same root as in deken (blanket) and dek (deck/lid). An ontdekking is literally an 'un-covering' — pulling back the cover to find something.

words.exampleSentence: De ontdekking van penicilline veranderde de geneeskunde.
words.exampleTranslation: The discovery of penicillin changed medicine.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here ver- is causative — it turns lang (long) into the act of making something longer. This is one of ver-'s most common roles in Dutch: turning an adjective into a verb of change.
From lang (long). The stem shifts slightly to leng- when combined with ver- and the -ing suffix, but the connection to lang (long) is direct and transparent.

words.translationLong: extension, prolongation, renewal (of contract/visa), extra time (sport)
words.exampleSentence: Hij vroeg om een verlenging van zijn visum.
words.exampleTranslation: He applied for an extension of his visa.

parts.homeLangDetails: The prefix be- here is causative and transitional — it directs the action outward onto an object. It transforms vast (firm, fixed) into an act of making something firm or affirming it.
From vast (firm, fixed, secure). The same root appears in vaststellen (to establish), vasthouden (to hold firm). Here the stem appears as vest- before the suffix.
A connecting element that helps form a verb from an adjective stem. Similar to the English -ify pattern — turning 'firm' into 'to make firm.'

words.translationLong: to confirm, to affirm, to verify, to fasten, to attach
words.exampleSentence: Kun jij bevestigen dat de vergadering om drie uur begint?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you confirm that the meeting starts at three o'clock?

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix that reverses or negates something. Here it undoes 'kennen' (to know/acknowledge) — to not-acknowledge, to deny.

words.translationLong: to deny, to negate, to disavow
words.exampleSentence: Hij blijft ontkennen dat hij de fout heeft gemaakt.
words.exampleTranslation: He keeps denying that he made the mistake.

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix meaning 'again' or 'anew'. Here it adds the sense of creating something over again or transforming it into something new.
The infinitive ending -pen here is the full form after a stem ending in -p; functionally equivalent to -en.

words.translationLong: to recreate, to transform, to reshape
words.exampleSentence: De kunstenaar wilde de ruimte volledig herscheppen.
words.exampleTranslation: The artist wanted to completely transform the space.

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix that here intensifies the idea of moving apart or away. It pushes 'wijd' (wide/far) into an action: to make something go far away, to remove it.
This -er is not the agent suffix but a comparative element — making something 'wider/farther'. Together with ver-, it creates the idea of actively distancing. Not the '-er' meaning 'one who does'.

words.translationLong: to remove, to delete, to eliminate
words.exampleSentence: Kun je dit bestand van mijn computer verwijderen?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you remove this file from my computer?

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix that makes an intransitive verb transitive — directing the action onto an object. Here it turns 'invloed' (influence, a noun) into a verb meaning to exert influence on something.

From 'vloeien' (to flow). 'Invloed' literally means an in-flowing — the same idea as English 'influence', which also comes from Latin fluere (to flow). The concept of influence as something that flows into you is shared by both languages.

words.exampleSentence: Muziek kan je stemming sterk beïnvloeden.
words.exampleTranslation: Music can strongly influence your mood.

parts.homeLangDetails: From 'tuig', meaning tackle, rigging, or gear — tools used to control or direct something. 'Overtuigen' literally suggests bringing someone over by means of compelling force or argument. The connection to physical tackle has faded; it now means simply to convince.

words.exampleSentence: Ze probeerde hem te overtuigen van haar gelijk.
words.exampleTranslation: She tried to convince him she was right.

parts.homeLangDetails: From 'ruim', meaning spacious or roomy. 'Opruimen' means to clear up space — to tidy, to put things away. The same root gives English 'room' (as in space, not just a bedroom).

words.translationLong: to tidy up, to clean up, to clear away
words.exampleSentence: Kun jij je kamer opruimen voor het avondeten?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you tidy up your room before dinner?

words.translationLong: to register, to enroll, to sign up
words.exampleSentence: Je moet je voor vrijdag inschrijven voor de cursus.
words.exampleTranslation: You need to register for the course by Friday.

words.translationLong: to unsubscribe, to deregister, to sign out
words.exampleSentence: Ik wil me uitschrijven voor deze nieuwsbrief.
words.exampleTranslation: I want to unsubscribe from this newsletter.

parts.homeLangDetails: A directional prefix suggesting approach or connection. Here it conveys directing yourself toward a system or authority — signing on, presenting yourself.

words.translationLong: to log in, to sign up, to register
words.exampleSentence: Je kunt je aanmelden met je e-mailadres.
words.exampleTranslation: You can sign up with your email address.

parts.homeLangDetails: A prefix indicating separation or completion of departure. Here it reverses 'aanmelden' — you are reporting yourself off, disconnecting or signing out.

words.translationLong: to log out, to sign off, to deregister
words.exampleSentence: Vergeet niet je af te melden als je klaar bent.
words.exampleTranslation: Don't forget to log out when you're done.

parts.homeLangDetails: From 'voegen', meaning to join or to fit together. Related to 'voeg' (a joint or seam in construction). 'Toevoegen' means to add something by joining it to what's already there.

words.translationLong: to add, to append, to attach
words.exampleSentence: Wil je je naam toevoegen aan de lijst?
words.exampleTranslation: Would you like to add your name to the list?

words.exampleSentence: Gooi dat oude brood maar weg.
words.exampleTranslation: Just throw that old bread away.

parts.homeLangDetails: Literally 'to strike' or 'to beat', but in compounds with 'op-' it takes on the meaning of storing or laying up — like stacking things up. The same shift happens in 'opslagplaats' (storage place). Think of it as 'laying up' rather than hitting.

words.translationLong: to save, to store, to stock
words.exampleSentence: Vergeet niet je document op te slaan.
words.exampleTranslation: Don't forget to save your document.
words.flag: only 2 parts — 'opslaan' is a separable verb where 'slaan' is the full infinitive form, so no separate infinitive suffix is added

words.exampleSentence: Je kunt de app gratis downloaden.
words.exampleTranslation: You can download the app for free.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here 'af-' gives the sense of producing a result or pressing something out — like pressing ink off onto paper. It makes 'drukken' (to press) into a completed, output-directed action.
The infinitive ending here is -ken rather than -en because the stem ends in -k; the full form is -ken to avoid doubling. Functionally the same as -en.

words.exampleSentence: Kun je dit formulier voor mij afdrukken?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you print this form for me?

words.translationLong: to set, to adjust, to configure, to establish
words.exampleSentence: Kun je de temperatuur instellen op twintig graden?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you set the temperature to twenty degrees?

parts.homeLangDetails: Here ver- conveys directed movement or sending — pointing something away toward somewhere else. Think of it as 'sending off' or 'directing away'.
From wijzen, meaning to point or to show. The same root appears in aanwijzen (to designate/point out) and uitwijzen (to expel/point out).

words.exampleSentence: De dokter gaf me een verwijzing naar een specialist.
words.exampleTranslation: The doctor gave me a referral to a specialist.

parts.homeLangDetails: From sluiten, meaning to close or to lock. Here it conveys the idea of clicking something shut — connecting or fastening things together.

words.exampleSentence: Ik heb de aansluiting op de trein gemist.
words.exampleTranslation: I missed the connecting train.

parts.homeLangDetails: From wisselen, meaning to exchange or to swap. The same root appears in wisseld geld (change/coins) and afwisseling (variety/alternation).

words.exampleSentence: De uitwisseling van studenten tussen de twee landen is erg waardevol.
words.exampleTranslation: The exchange of students between the two countries is very valuable.

words.translationLong: to spend time, to pass time
words.exampleSentence: We gaan de vakantie doorbrengen in Spanje.
words.exampleTranslation: We are going to spend the holiday in Spain.

words.translationLong: to grow up, to come of age
words.exampleSentence: Ze is opgegroeid in een klein dorp.
words.exampleTranslation: She grew up in a small village.

parts.homeLangDetails: From laten, meaning to let or to leave. The same root appears in overlaten (to leave to someone) and nalaten (to leave behind/neglect).

words.exampleSentence: Hij heeft zijn sleutels thuis achtergelaten.
words.exampleTranslation: He left his keys at home.

words.translationLong: to cooperate, to collaborate, to work along with
words.exampleSentence: Alle medewerkers moeten meewerken aan het nieuwe project.
words.exampleTranslation: All employees must cooperate on the new project.

words.translationLong: to give back, to return, to hand back
words.exampleSentence: Vergeet niet het boek aan de bibliotheek terug te geven.
words.exampleTranslation: Don't forget to return the book to the library.

parts.homeLangDetails: From bereiden, meaning to prepare or to make ready. It is related to the adjective bereid (ready, willing). Think of it as 'making something ready'.

words.exampleSentence: Ze moet zich goed voorbereiden op het examen.
words.exampleTranslation: She needs to prepare well for the exam.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here af- gives the verb a sense of finality or mutual agreement — settling something between parties. Think of it as 'speaking something off' to a conclusion.

words.translationLong: to agree on, to arrange, to make an appointment
words.exampleSentence: Laten we een tijd afspreken voor de vergadering.
words.exampleTranslation: Let's agree on a time for the meeting.

parts.homeLangDetails: This root is shared between Dutch and English and appears in many compound nouns describing activities or structures. "Werk" on its own means work, and it combines freely: "huiswerk" (homework), "kunstwerk" (artwork), "samenwerken" (to work together). Recognizing it instantly unlocks a large family of everyday words.

words.exampleSentence: De timmerman heeft een grote werkplaats achter zijn huis.
words.exampleTranslation: The carpenter has a large workshop behind his house.

parts.homeLangDetails: Another direct cognate with English, "huis" means house and appears as a compounding root in dozens of common nouns. "Ziekenhuis" (hospital, literally sick-house), "postkantoor" shares the building idea, "warenhuis" (department store). Dutch loves compound nouns and "huis" is one of the most frequent building blocks you will encounter.

words.exampleSentence: Mijn huisdier is een kat die Mies heet.
words.exampleTranslation: My pet is a cat named Mies.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here 'goed' means 'stuff' or 'goods' in the collective sense — things you own or use. It's the same word as 'good' (adjective) but used here as a noun meaning belongings or wares, as in 'handelsgoed' (trade goods).

words.exampleSentence: De kinderen lieten hun speelgoed overal slingeren.
words.exampleTranslation: The children left their toys scattered everywhere.

words.exampleSentence: Mijn werkgever vergoedt de reiskosten.
words.exampleTranslation: My employer reimburses the travel expenses.

parts.homeLangDetails: The plural form of 'zieke' (sick person), used here as a linking element. So 'ziekenhuis' literally means 'house for sick people.' The -en- here functions as a linking element connecting the two parts of the compound.

Another direct cognate with English, "huis" means house and appears as a compounding root in dozens of common nouns. "Ziekenhuis" (hospital, literally sick-house), "postkantoor" shares the building idea, "warenhuis" (department store). Dutch loves compound nouns and "huis" is one of the most frequent building blocks you will encounter.

words.exampleSentence: Hij werd met spoed naar het ziekenhuis gebracht.
words.exampleTranslation: He was rushed to the hospital.

parts.homeLangDetails: The plural (or linking) form of 'kind' (child). Used here as the first element of a compound, as in 'kinderen' (children). The same form appears in 'kinderkamer' (children's room).

words.exampleSentence: Ze duwde de kinderwagen door het park.
words.exampleTranslation: She pushed the pram through the park.

words.exampleSentence: Ze vulde de boodschappenwagen met groenten en fruit.
words.exampleTranslation: She filled the shopping cart with vegetables and fruit.
words.flag: only 2 parts but 'boodschappen' is itself a compound (boodschap + -en plural); flagging as boodschappen is treated as a single unit here

words.exampleSentence: Ze schrijft elke avond in haar dagboek.
words.exampleTranslation: She writes in her diary every evening.

words.exampleSentence: Gebruik het broodmes om de baguette te snijden.
words.exampleTranslation: Use the bread knife to cut the baguette.

words.exampleSentence: In Nederland is er overal een goed fietspad.
words.exampleTranslation: In the Netherlands there is a good cycle path everywhere.

parts.homeLangDetails: Yes, this is the same word as 'shoe' — a glove is literally a 'hand-shoe' in Dutch (and in German: Handschuh). The logic is that a glove covers and protects the hand just as a shoe covers the foot.

words.exampleSentence: Trek je handschoenen aan, het is erg koud buiten.
words.exampleTranslation: Put on your gloves, it is very cold outside.

words.exampleSentence: Haar kledingkast zit vol met jurken en jassen.
words.exampleTranslation: Her wardrobe is full of dresses and coats.

words.exampleSentence: De badkamer heeft een douche en een bad.
words.exampleTranslation: The bathroom has a shower and a bathtub.

words.exampleSentence: We eten elke ochtend aan de keukentafel.
words.exampleTranslation: We eat at the kitchen table every morning.

parts.homeLangDetails: The plural of 'tand' (tooth), used as the first element of this compound. The -en ending is a standard Dutch plural, but here it also acts as a linking element joining the two parts of the compound.

words.exampleSentence: Vergeet niet twee keer per dag je tandenborstel te gebruiken.
words.exampleTranslation: Don't forget to use your toothbrush twice a day.

parts.homeLangDetails: Tuig means equipment, rigging, or gear — and by extension, a vehicle or craft. It appears in other Dutch words like 'rijtuig' (carriage) and 'vaartuig' (vessel). Here it combines with 'vlieg' (fly) to mean the craft you fly in.

words.exampleSentence: Het vliegtuig landt over tien minuten.
words.exampleTranslation: The airplane lands in ten minutes.

words.exampleSentence: We spreken af bij het treinstation om drie uur.
words.exampleTranslation: We'll meet at the train station at three o'clock.

parts.homeLangDetails: Halte comes from the verb 'halteren' (to halt, to stop) and refers to a designated stopping point. It's the same root as English 'halt' — a place where something comes to a stop.

words.exampleSentence: De bushalte is vlak om de hoek.
words.exampleTranslation: The bus stop is just around the corner.

parts.homeLangDetails: In Dutch compound nouns, an "-s-" is often inserted between two parts purely to aid pronunciation, with no meaning of its own. It is similar to the linking "-s-" in English words like "sportsman." You see it in "rijbewijs" but more clearly in "regeringsgebouw" (government building) or "tijdschrift" (magazine, literally time-writing). Do not try to interpret it as a genitive or plural -- it is just glue.

Another direct cognate with English, "huis" means house and appears as a compounding root in dozens of common nouns. "Ziekenhuis" (hospital, literally sick-house), "postkantoor" shares the building idea, "warenhuis" (department store). Dutch loves compound nouns and "huis" is one of the most frequent building blocks you will encounter.

words.exampleSentence: De trouwceremonie vond plaats in het stadshuis.
words.exampleTranslation: The wedding ceremony took place in city hall.

words.exampleSentence: Op zaterdag is er altijd een markt op de marktplaats.
words.exampleTranslation: On Saturdays there is always a market at the marketplace.

parts.homeLangDetails: Just like English "-ing" used as a noun (a meeting, a building), Dutch "-ing" turns verbs into nouns describing an action or its result. Examples: "vergadering" (meeting), "regeling" (arrangement, regulation), "opleiding" (education, training). This is one of the most frequent noun-forming suffixes in Dutch and you will bump into it on every page of text.

words.exampleSentence: Kun jij hier je handtekening zetten?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you put your signature here?

parts.homeLangDetails: Geboorte is the Dutch noun for birth. It comes from 'geboren' (born), which itself uses the ge- past-participle prefix on the root 'boren' (to bear/bring forth). So it literally means 'the born-ness' — the event of being born.

words.exampleSentence: Wanneer is jouw geboortedag?
words.exampleTranslation: When is your birthday?

parts.homeLangDetails: Gebouw comes from 'bouwen' (to build) with the ge- prefix, which here functions nominalizing — forming a noun from a verb to mean the result of building. It is not a past participle marker in the usual sense; it's a noun meaning 'what has been built.'

words.exampleSentence: Het nieuwe schoolgebouw werd vorig jaar geopend.
words.exampleTranslation: The new school building was opened last year.

parts.homeLangDetails: Regering comes from 'regeren' (to govern, to rule) plus the -ing suffix, giving 'the act or body of governing.' Compare English 'reign' — both trace back to Latin regere (to rule).

In Dutch compound nouns, an "-s-" is often inserted between two parts purely to aid pronunciation, with no meaning of its own. It is similar to the linking "-s-" in English words like "sportsman." You see it in "rijbewijs" but more clearly in "regeringsgebouw" (government building) or "tijdschrift" (magazine, literally time-writing). Do not try to interpret it as a genitive or plural -- it is just glue.

Leider comes from 'leiden' (to lead) plus the -er suffix meaning 'one who does.' It is a transparent agent noun: 'one who leads.' Note that 'leider' also means 'unfortunately' in Dutch — a completely unrelated homonym.

words.exampleSentence: De regeringsleider hield een toespraak voor het parlement.
words.exampleTranslation: The government leader gave a speech before parliament.

parts.homeLangDetails: This is the go-to suffix for forming abstract nouns from adjectives, directly mirroring English "-ness." It is completely regular and highly productive: "vrijheid" (freedom, literally free-ness), "schoonheid" (beauty), "eerlijkheid" (honesty). Whenever you want to name a quality or state in Dutch, slap "-heid" on the adjective and you are almost certainly right.

You see it in "rijbewijs" but more clearly in "regeringsgebouw" (government building) or "tijdschrift" (magazine, literally time-writing). Do not try to interpret it as a genitive or plural -- it is just glue.

words.exampleSentence: Doe je veiligheidsriem om voordat de auto rijdt.
words.exampleTranslation: Put on your safety belt before the car moves.

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix turns intransitive verbs or nouns into transitive verbs, similar to how English sometimes uses "be-" in words like "befriend." It often adds a sense of acting on something: "betalen" (to pay), "bezoeken" (to visit), "beginnen" (to begin). It is extremely common and gives many Dutch verbs their characteristic shape.

From 'leiden' (to lead, to guide). The verb 'begeleiden' means to accompany or guide someone — 'be-' makes the action directed at someone, and 'leiden' provides the core meaning of leading or guiding.

These two variants form agent nouns, exactly like English "-er" in "teacher" or "worker." The choice between them depends on the verb ending, but the meaning is always the same: "leraar" (teacher), "werker" (worker), "bakker" (baker). If you see a Dutch noun ending in "-er" or "-aar," there is a good chance it names a person defined by what they do.

words.exampleSentence: De begeleider leidde de groep door het museum.
words.exampleTranslation: The guide led the group through the museum.

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix turns intransitive verbs or nouns into transitive verbs, similar to how English sometimes uses "be-" in words like "befriend." It often adds a sense of acting on something: "betalen" (to pay), "bezoeken" (to visit), "beginnen" (to begin). It is extremely common and gives many Dutch verbs their characteristic shape.

From 'waken' (to watch over, to guard, to be awake). The same root gives English 'wake' and 'watch' — being alert and keeping an eye on something. 'Bewaken' means to guard or keep watch over something.

These two variants form agent nouns, exactly like English "-er" in "teacher" or "worker." The choice between them depends on the verb ending, but the meaning is always the same: "leraar" (teacher), "werker" (worker), "bakker" (baker). If you see a Dutch noun ending in "-er" or "-aar," there is a good chance it names a person defined by what they do.

words.exampleSentence: De bewaker controleerde de identiteitskaarten bij de ingang.
words.exampleTranslation: The guard checked the identity cards at the entrance.

parts.homeLangDetails: Kracht means strength, force, or power. In compound words like 'leerkracht' and 'werkkracht' (workforce), it takes on the sense of a capable person — someone who wields power or ability in a given domain. Think of it as 'a force' in a field.

words.exampleSentence: Onze leerkracht legt altijd alles heel duidelijk uit.
words.exampleTranslation: Our teacher always explains everything very clearly.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -ster is a feminine agent suffix in Dutch, equivalent to -er but marking the person as female. So 'schrijver' is a male or gender-neutral writer, while 'schrijfster' is specifically a female writer. The same pattern applies to 'spreekster' (female speaker).

words.exampleSentence: De schrijfster signeerde haar nieuwe roman na de lezing.
words.exampleTranslation: The female writer signed her new novel after the reading.

parts.homeLangDetails: The suffix -ster is a feminine agent suffix in Dutch, equivalent to -er but marking the person as female. So 'spreker' is a male or gender-neutral speaker, while 'spreekster' is specifically a female speaker. The same pattern appears in 'schrijfster' (female writer).

words.exampleSentence: De spreekster hield een inspirerende toespraak over gelijkheid.
words.exampleTranslation: The female speaker gave an inspiring speech about equality.

words.exampleSentence: Hij is de bezitter van een groot huis.
words.exampleTranslation: He is the owner of a large house.

From oefenen (to practice, to exercise). The same root appears in oefening (exercise, drill) and inoefenen (to rehearse). Here be- turns the noun 'oefening' into a transitive verbal idea — one who actively practices something.

words.exampleSentence: Ze is een ervaren beoefenaar van yoga.
words.exampleTranslation: She is an experienced practitioner of yoga.

parts.homeLangDetails: A key Dutch cultural word — the full adjective gezellig means something like cozy, convivial, or sociably warm. It comes from gezel (companion, fellow), so it literally evokes the quality of being good company. There is no perfect English equivalent, which is why the noun gezelligheid is often borrowed untranslated.

This is the go-to suffix for forming abstract nouns from adjectives, directly mirroring English "-ness." It is completely regular and highly productive: "vrijheid" (freedom, literally free-ness), "schoonheid" (beauty), "eerlijkheid" (honesty). Whenever you want to name a quality or state in Dutch, slap "-heid" on the adjective and you are almost certainly right.

words.translationLong: coziness, conviviality, the quality of being warm and sociable
words.exampleSentence: De gezelligheid van de avond maakte iedereen blij.
words.exampleTranslation: The coziness of the evening made everyone happy.

parts.homeLangDetails: The adjective moeilijk (difficult, hard) is the base here. Its deeper etymology is uncertain, but for learners it is best treated as a root meaning 'difficult.' The -lijk ending is normally a suffix meaning -ly/-like, but in moeilijk it is fully fused and moeilijk functions as a single adjective unit.

This is the go-to suffix for forming abstract nouns from adjectives, directly mirroring English "-ness." It is completely regular and highly productive: "vrijheid" (freedom, literally free-ness), "schoonheid" (beauty), "eerlijkheid" (honesty). Whenever you want to name a quality or state in Dutch, slap "-heid" on the adjective and you are almost certainly right.

words.exampleSentence: Hij had grote moeilijkheid met de wiskundeopgave.
words.exampleTranslation: He had great difficulty with the math problem.

parts.homeLangDetails: The adjective helder means clear, bright, or lucid — used for clear water, bright light, or a clear explanation. The -er here is part of the root adjective, not an agent or comparative suffix.

This is the go-to suffix for forming abstract nouns from adjectives, directly mirroring English "-ness." It is completely regular and highly productive: "vrijheid" (freedom, literally free-ness), "schoonheid" (beauty), "eerlijkheid" (honesty). Whenever you want to name a quality or state in Dutch, slap "-heid" on the adjective and you are almost certainly right.

words.exampleSentence: De helderheid van zijn uitleg was verfrissend.
words.exampleTranslation: The clarity of his explanation was refreshing.

parts.homeLangDetails: The adjective zeker means certain, sure, or safe. It is related to German sicher and ultimately to Latin securus. The -er is part of the root, not a suffix.

This is the go-to suffix for forming abstract nouns from adjectives, directly mirroring English "-ness." It is completely regular and highly productive: "vrijheid" (freedom, literally free-ness), "schoonheid" (beauty), "eerlijkheid" (honesty). Whenever you want to name a quality or state in Dutch, slap "-heid" on the adjective and you are almost certainly right.

words.exampleSentence: Ze sprak met grote zekerheid over de toekomst.
words.exampleTranslation: She spoke with great certainty about the future.

parts.homeLangDetails: The adjective bereid means ready or willing. It is related to the verb bereiden (to prepare), but in modern Dutch bereid functions independently as 'willing, prepared to.' Think of it as the state of being ready for something.

words.exampleSentence: Zijn bereidheid om te helpen werd erg gewaardeerd.
words.exampleTranslation: His willingness to help was greatly appreciated.

parts.homeLangDetails: The adjective tevreden means satisfied or content. It comes from te (to) + vreden, an archaic form of vrede (peace). So it literally means 'at peace with' — satisfied, not wanting more. The te- here is a frozen preposition, not the infinitive marker.

words.exampleSentence: Er is veel tevredenheid onder de medewerkers.
words.exampleTranslation: There is a lot of satisfaction among the employees.

words.exampleSentence: De leesbaarheid van de tekst kan worden verbeterd door kortere zinnen.
